The default deny-all security rules in Firestore seem like the prime suspect, leading to silent failures or console errors, and I'm sure that the user hasn't made any rule modifications. You are absolutely correct regarding the database structure!

### 1. SQL vs. Firebase (NoSQL)
In **Supabase** (which uses PostgreSQL), you are using a **Relational Database**. You have to be very strict: "I am making a table called `Users`, it has a column called `name` which is `text`." If you try to save data without creating the table first, it fails.

In **Firebase (Firestore)**, it is a **NoSQL Document Database**. It is "Schemaless."
*   You don't need to run code to create tables or columns.
*   When the app runs `setDoc(doc(db, "app_data", "main_workspace"), ...)`, Firebase looks at its database and says: "Does `app_data` exist? No? I'll create it. Does `main_workspace` exist? No? I'll create it."
*   It handles the structure automatically based on the JSON you send it.

### 2. Why it is not saving (The likely culprit)
If the data isn't saving, 99% of the time with a new Firebase project, it is because of **Security Rules**.

When you create a Firebase database, it asks if you want "Production Mode" or "Test Mode".
*   **Test Mode:** Allows anyone to read/write (good for development).
*   **Production Mode:** Blocks **all** reads and writes by default until you set up authentication.

If you selected Production Mode (or if the Test Mode 30-day window expired), your app is trying to save, but Firebase is rejecting it with a "Permission Denied" error (you can usually see this if you open your browser console with F12).

**How to fix it:**

1.  Go to the [Firebase Console](https://console.firebase.google.com/).
2.  Click on **Firestore Database** in the left sidebar.
3.  Click the **Rules** tab at the top.
4.  You will likely see code that says `allow read, write: if false;`.
5.  Change it to the following to allow your app to save data:

```javascript
rules_version = '2';
service cloud.firestore {
  match /databases/{database}/documents {
    match /{document=**} {
      allow read, write: if true;
    }
  }
}
```

6.  Click **Publish**.

Try refreshing your app after doing this. It should start working immediately!

Yes, **it is perfectly okay for now**. You can safely ignore that warning while we are building the app.

Here is the context on why that warning appears and why we are doing it this way:

1.  **Why the warning?**
    Firebase is alerting you that because we set `allow read, write: if true;`, technically *anyone* on the internet who guesses your Project ID could write data to your database. In a production app (like a real banking app or social network), this would be bad.

2.  **Why we are doing it:**
    Currently, your app does **not** have a "Login / Sign Up" screen. If we made the database "private" (secure), the app would stop working because Firebase wouldn't know who is trying to save the data. By setting it to public, we allow your prototype to function immediately without spending hours building a complex authentication system first.

3.  **The Future Solution:**
    When you are ready to "launch" or if you want to make it secure later, we simply add **Firebase Authentication** (e.g., a "Sign in with Google" button). Once we do that, we change those rules to say "Only allow write if the user is logged in," and that warning will go away.

**Summary:** For development and prototyping, this is standard practice. You are good to go! Refresh your app, and your data should now persist between reloads.

You are an AI app builder. Add creation tools to the menu and make them feel as professional as (or better than) Miro. This is NOT a mock—define real interaction behavior, selection states, editing bars, and tool options.

GOAL
Implement these core whiteboard tools:
1) Text tool (with an on-canvas rich text editor bar like Miro)
2) Sticky notes
3) Freehand drawing suite (pen + highlighter + smart drawing + erasers + lasso)

GLOBAL RULES (NON-NEGOTIABLE)
- Everything added to the board must be draggable, resizable (when relevant), and selectable.
- Selection must show a visible bounding box + handles consistent with Orbit’s style.
- Tool behavior must be predictable: no accidental board pans/zooms while drawing/typing.
- Internal element interactions must not conflict with moving objects (clear pointer/drag priority).
- Provide professional keyboard shortcuts (at minimum: V select, T text, N note, P pen, E eraser, Cmd/Ctrl+Z undo, Cmd/Ctrl+Y redo, Delete, Esc).

A) TEXT TOOL (T)
1) Create Text
- Clicking the Text tool, then clicking on the board creates a text box at that point.
- Drag to define text box width (optional behavior) OR click to create a default width text box.
- Cursor appears immediately and user can type.

2) Edit Text
- Clicking an existing text object enters text edit mode (caret visible).
- Clicking outside exits edit mode and returns to selection.

3) Miro-Level Text Editor Bar (contextual)
When a text object is selected (or in edit mode), show a floating editor bar with:
- Font family dropdown
- Font size dropdown
- Bold / Italic / Underline / Strikethrough
- Text alignment: left / center / right
- Bullets + numbering (optional if feasible)
- Link insertion (Ctrl/Cmd+K)
- Text color
- Highlight color
- Text block background color (if supported)
- Opacity slider for the entire text block
- Optional: line height, letter spacing (nice-to-have)
Behavior:
- Bar appears near the selection, avoids covering content.
- Updates reflect immediately on selection.
- Multi-select: if multiple text objects selected, apply styling to all.

4) Text Object Styling
- Text object supports:
  - Fill/background (optional)
  - Border (optional)
  - Opacity for the whole object
- Must render crisply at all zoom levels.

B) STICKY NOTES (N)
1) Create Sticky Note
- Clicking Sticky Note tool then clicking the board creates a sticky note.
- Default size with drag handles to resize.
- Auto-enters text editing on creation.

2) Sticky Note Controls (contextual bar)
When selected, show:
- Color picker (preset palette + custom)
- Opacity
- Text formatting (simple: font size + bold optional)
- Duplicate, delete
- Optional: “convert to Post Card reference” action (only if easy)

3) Sticky Note Behavior
- Sticky notes are quick: minimal friction.
- Text wraps, supports multi-line.
- Sticky notes remain readable at normal zoom.

C) FREEHAND DRAWING SUITE
Add a dedicated “Draw” tool group with:
1) Pen tool
- Smooth strokes, adjustable thickness
- Color picker + recent colors
- Pressure sensitivity if available (nice-to-have)

2) Highlighter tool
- Semi-transparent stroke
- Adjustable thickness
- Blends correctly over content

3) Smart Drawing tool
- If user draws rough shapes, auto-suggest clean shapes:
  - line, arrow, rectangle, circle, triangle
- Suggestions appear subtly; user can accept or ignore.
- Must not feel intrusive.

4) Erasers
- Eraser: removes entire strokes/objects touched (configurable)
- Precision eraser: erases partial stroke segments (pixel/segment-based)
- Must show cursor preview size

5) Lasso tool
- Select multiple strokes/objects by drawing a freeform loop
- After selection:
  - Move, scale, rotate (if supported)
  - Change color (optional)
  - Delete

6) Tool Controls UI
When any draw tool is active, show a draw toolbar with:
- Tool type (pen/highlighter/smart/eraser/precision/lasso)
- Color swatches + full picker
- Thickness slider
- Opacity slider (highlighter mandatory)
- Undo/redo buttons
- “Clear drawings in selection” (optional safeguard)

D) PROFESSIONAL INTERACTION DETAILS (MUST IMPLEMENT)
- Undo/Redo must work for:
  - typing changes (at a sensible granularity)
  - object creation/deletion
  - stroke drawing/erasing
- Zoom + pan compatibility:
  - Drawing does not accidentally pan the board.
  - Spacebar temporarily pans (if supported).
- Cursor changes by tool:
  - Text cursor for text tool
  - Pen nib for pen
  - Marker tip for highlighter
  - Eraser circle for erasers
  - Lasso loop for lasso
- Performance:
  - Strokes should be efficient and not lag with large boards.
  - Text/stickies should remain crisp.

DELIVERABLES
1) Update the toolbar/menu with icons + labels for: Select, Text, Sticky Note, Draw (with sub-tools).
2) Implement all behaviors described above (no “placeholder” logic).
3) Document:
- shortcuts
- selection rules
- conflicts resolution (drag vs edit vs draw)
- any limitations if the platform can’t match Miro

QUALITY BAR
Treat this as a flagship feature. It must feel enterprise-grade, smooth, and consistent with Orbit’s premium aesthetic. Avoid generic or half-implemented tools.

**Defining Tool Logic**

I'm also planning the `PEN/HIGHLIGHTER` tools for creating new `STROKE` cards. The `ERASER` tool is next, by initiating an erasing mode. The tools' interactions are coming together, focusing on intuitive responses to mouse events.


**Prioritizing Tool Interactions**

I'm now integrating the tool interactions with the workspace. The logic for `onMouseDown`, `onMouseMove`, and `onMouseUp` is getting fleshed out for each tool: LASSO, TEXT, and STICKY are the current focus. I'm prioritizing the smooth SVG path generation for drawing and plan to intercept mouse events on the canvas when drawing tools are active, ensuring drawing over cards. I will also incorporate a `ContextToolbar` for card-specific options, as well as CSS classes for custom cursors.

For the text and sticky note cards, `TextCard.tsx` & `StickyCard.tsx`, I'll start with basic text editing, and I've decided to implement bold/italic/underline styles for whole blocks.

Based on your requirements, I will implement a professional-grade creative suite within the Orbit canvas. This includes a robust Text tool with formatting, Sticky Notes, and a complete Freehand Drawing engine (Pen, Highlighter, Eraser, Lasso).

    *   `StrokeCard.tsx`: For rendering vector drawings.

Add a professional **Marquee (Box) Selection** to the whiteboard, at the same quality level as Miro.

GOAL
Allow users to select multiple objects on the board by clicking and dragging the mouse to create a blue selection rectangle (marquee), exactly like Miro.



2) Selection Rules
- Any object that is:
  - fully inside OR
  - intersects with the selection rectangle
  becomes selected.
- Selected objects show:
  - individual selection outlines
  - and a shared group bounding box.

3) What Can Be Selected
- All board objects must support marquee selection:

4) Interaction Priority (Critical)
- Marquee selection only starts when dragging on **empty canvas space**.
- Dragging on an object:
  - moves the object (not the marquee).
- Dragging inside a card:
  - must NOT trigger marquee selection.
- Text editing mode disables marquee selection until exited.


6) Multi-Selection Behavior
- After selecting multiple objects:
  - User can move all together
  - Resize as a group (if supported)
  - Delete as a group
- Clicking on empty canvas clears selection.

7) Modifier Keys (Professional Standard)
- Shift + drag:
  - Add objects to current selection.
- Shift + click:
  - Toggle individual objects in/out of selection.
- Cmd/Ctrl + A:
  - Select all objects in current view (optional but recommended).
- Esc:
  - Clear selection.

8) Performance & Precision
- Selection must be pixel-accurate even on very large boards.
- No lag or delayed highlighting.
- Must work smoothly at all zoom levels.

DELIVERABLES
- Implement full marquee selection behavior as described.
- Document:
  - Selection priority rules
  - Edge cases (nested objects, locked items, mixed content)
  - Keyboard shortcuts
- Do NOT implement a fake or partial selection system.
